5	//! Gallops type definitions
6	//!
7	//! Core data structures for Job Jockey heat and pace tracking.
8	
9	use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};
10	use std::collections::BTreeMap;
11	
12	/// Unknown/default basis SHA (7 zeros)
13	///
14	/// Used when basis commit cannot be determined (git errors).
15	/// All basis-related code derives the expected length from this constant.
16	pub const JJRG_UNKNOWN_BASIS: &str = "0000000";
17	
18	/// Pace state values
19	#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize, Deserialize)]
20	pub enum jjrg_PaceState {
21	    #[serde(rename = "jjgte_rough")]
22	    Rough,
23	    #[serde(rename = "jjgte_bridled", alias = "primed")]
24	    Bridled,
25	    #[serde(rename = "jjgte_complete")]
26	    Complete,
27	    #[serde(rename = "jjgte_abandoned")]
28	    Abandoned,
29	}
30	
31	/// Heat status values
32	#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize, Deserialize)]
33	pub enum jjrg_HeatStatus {
34	    /// Heat is actively being worked
35	    #[serde(rename = "jjghe_racing")]
36	    Racing,
37	    /// Heat is paused, not actively worked
38	    #[serde(rename = "jjghe_stabled")]
39	    Stabled,
40	    /// Heat is complete and archived (terminal state)
41	    #[serde(rename = "jjghe_retired")]
42	    Retired,
43	}
44	
45	/// Tack record - snapshot of Pace state and plan
46	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
47	pub struct jjrg_Tack {
48	    #[serde(rename = "jjgtn_ts")]
49	    pub ts: String,
50	    #[serde(rename = "jjgtn_state")]
51	    pub state: jjrg_PaceState,
52	    #[serde(rename = "jjgtn_text")]
53	    pub text: String,
54	    #[serde(rename = "jjgtn_silks")]
55	    pub silks: String,
56	    #[serde(rename = "jjgtn_basis")]
57	    pub basis: String,
58	    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none", rename = "jjgtn_direction")]
59	    pub direction: Option<String>,
60	}
61	
62	/// Pace record - discrete action within a Heat
63	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
64	pub struct jjrg_Pace {
65	    #[serde(rename = "jjgpn_tacks")]
66	    pub tacks: Vec<jjrg_Tack>,
67	}
68	
69	/// Initial pensum seed value (2-char base64url, starts at "AA")
70	pub const JJRT_PENSUM_SEED_INIT: &str = "AA";
71	
72	/// Heat record - bounded initiative
73	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
74	pub struct jjrg_Heat {
75	    #[serde(rename = "jjghn_silks")]
76	    pub silks: String,
77	    #[serde(rename = "jjghn_creation_time")]
78	    pub creation_time: String,
79	    #[serde(rename = "jjghn_status")]
80	    pub status: jjrg_HeatStatus,
81	    #[serde(rename = "jjghn_order")]
82	    pub order: Vec<String>,
83	    #[serde(rename = "jjghn_next_pace_seed")]
84	    pub next_pace_seed: String,
85	    #[serde(rename = "jjghn_paces")]
86	    pub paces: BTreeMap<String, jjrg_Pace>,
87	}
88	
89	/// Root Gallops structure
90	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
91	pub struct jjrg_Gallops {
92	    #[serde(default, rename = "jjgrn_schema_version")]
93	    pub schema_version: Option<u32>,
94	    #[serde(rename = "jjgrn_next_heat_seed")]
95	    pub next_heat_seed: String,
96	    #[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = "Vec::is_empty", rename = "jjgrn_heat_order")]
97	    pub heat_order: Vec<String>,
98	    #[serde(rename = "jjgrn_heats")]
99	    pub heats: BTreeMap<String, jjrg_Heat>,
100	}
101	
102	/// Arguments for the nominate operation
103	pub struct jjrg_NominateArgs {
104	    pub silks: String,
105	    pub created: String,
106	}
107	
108	/// Result of the nominate operation
109	#[derive(Debug)]
110	pub struct jjrg_NominateResult {
111	    pub firemark: String,
112	}
113	
114	/// Arguments for the slate operation
115	pub struct jjrg_SlateArgs {
116	    pub firemark: String,
117	    pub silks: String,
118	    pub text: String,
119	    /// Coronet to insert before (mutually exclusive with after/first)
120	    pub before: Option<String>,
121	    /// Coronet to insert after (mutually exclusive with before/first)
122	    pub after: Option<String>,
123	    /// Insert at beginning (mutually exclusive with before/after)
124	    pub first: bool,
125	}
126	
127	/// Result of the slate operation
128	#[derive(Debug)]
129	pub struct jjrg_SlateResult {
130	    pub coronet: String,
131	}
132	
133	/// Arguments for the rail operation
134	///
135	/// Supports two modes:
136	/// - Order mode: provide `order` array to replace entire sequence
137	/// - Move mode: provide `move_coronet` + one positioning field to relocate a single pace
138	pub struct jjrg_RailArgs {
139	    pub firemark: String,
140	    /// Order mode: new sequence of all coronets
141	    pub order: Vec<String>,
142	    /// Move mode: coronet to relocate
143	    pub move_coronet: Option<String>,
144	    /// Move before this coronet
145	    pub before: Option<String>,
146	    /// Move after this coronet
147	    pub after: Option<String>,
148	    /// Move to beginning
149	    pub first: bool,
150	    /// Move to end
151	    pub last: bool,
152	}
153	
154	/// Arguments for the tally operation
155	pub struct jjrg_TallyArgs {
156	    pub coronet: String,
157	    pub state: Option<jjrg_PaceState>,
158	    pub direction: Option<String>,
159	    pub text: Option<String>,
160	    pub silks: Option<String>,
161	}
162	
163	/// Arguments for the draft operation
164	pub struct jjrg_DraftArgs {
165	    /// Coronet of the pace to move
166	    pub coronet: String,
167	    /// Destination heat Firemark
168	    pub to: String,
169	    /// Coronet to insert before (mutually exclusive with after/first)
170	    pub before: Option<String>,
171	    /// Coronet to insert after (mutually exclusive with before/first)
172	    pub after: Option<String>,
173	    /// Insert at beginning (mutually exclusive with before/after)
174	    pub first: bool,
175	}
176	
177	/// Result of the draft operation
178	#[derive(Debug)]
179	pub struct jjrg_DraftResult {
180	    /// New coronet in destination heat
181	    pub new_coronet: String,
182	}
183	
184	/// Arguments for the retire operation
185	pub struct jjrg_RetireArgs {
186	    /// Firemark of heat to retire
187	    pub firemark: String,
188	    /// Today's date in YYMMDD format (for trophy filename)
189	    pub today: String,
190	}
191	
192	/// Result of the retire operation
193	#[derive(Debug)]
194	pub struct jjrg_RetireResult {
195	    /// Path to created trophy file
196	    pub trophy_path: String,
197	    /// Path to deleted paddock file
198	    pub paddock_path: String,
199	    /// Heat silks (for commit message)
200	    pub silks: String,
201	    /// Firemark display string (for commit message)
202	    pub firemark: String,
203	}
204	
205	/// Arguments for the furlough operation
206	pub struct jjrg_FurloughArgs {
207	    /// Firemark of heat to furlough
208	    pub firemark: String,
209	    /// Set status to racing (mutually exclusive with stabled)
210	    pub racing: bool,
211	    /// Set status to stabled (mutually exclusive with racing)
212	    pub stabled: bool,
213	    /// New silks (rename heat)
214	    pub silks: Option<String>,
215	}
216	
217	/// Arguments for the restring operation
218	pub struct jjrg_RestringArgs {
219	    /// Source heat Firemark
220	    pub source_firemark: String,
221	    /// Destination heat Firemark
222	    pub dest_firemark: String,
223	    /// Coronets to transfer (in order)
224	    pub coronets: Vec<String>,
225	}
226	
227	/// Mapping of old coronet to new coronet with pace metadata
228	#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
229	pub struct jjrg_RestringMapping {
230	    pub old_coronet: String,
231	    pub new_coronet: String,
232	    pub silks: String,
233	    pub state: jjrg_PaceState,
234	    pub spec: String,
235	}
236	
237	/// Result of the restring operation
238	#[derive(Debug)]
239	pub struct jjrg_RestringResult {
240	    /// Source heat info
241	    pub source_firemark: String,
242	    pub source_silks: String,
243	    pub source_paddock: String,
244	    pub source_empty_after: bool,
245	    /// Destination heat info
246	    pub dest_firemark: String,
247	    pub dest_silks: String,
248	    pub dest_paddock: String,
249	    /// Drafted pace mappings (in transfer order)
250	    pub drafted: Vec<jjrg_RestringMapping>,
251	}
252	
253	/// Context returned by resolve_pace — snapshot of current pace state
254	#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
255	pub struct jjrg_PaceContext {
256	    /// Coronet display key
257	    pub coronet_key: String,
258	    /// Parent firemark display key
259	    pub firemark_key: String,
260	    /// Current pace state
261	    pub state: jjrg_PaceState,
262	    /// Current docket text
263	    pub text: String,
264	    /// Current silks
265	    pub silks: String,
266	    /// Current direction (if bridled)
267	    pub direction: Option<String>,
268	}
269

lastPrompt: Serious Job Jockey architecture discussion — decoupling JJ from the work repo.    Today JJ is married into each work repo (gallops committed alongside the code).   I want to move to: a central, single…

aiTitle: Decouple Job Jockey from work repositories

content.text: I have the persistence core and the foray vocabulary. Two more things are load-bearing for your specific questions before I opine: the **`basis` field** (JJ already captures a work-repo SHA per tack — this is the existing precedent for what you're proposing), and the **commit-lock mechanism** itself (the concurrency question). Let me pull both, plus the foray plant/relay operation bodies.

content.text:
I've loaded the load-bearing pieces: the persistence triad (load/save/persist), the `basis` field, the foray/legatio model with its curia-readiness guard, the officium/commit-lock concurrency model, and the lock implementation itself (`refs/vvg/locks/vvx`, a fail-fast create-only CAS — no blocking, no retry in the acquire path; I did *not* trace whether retry/backoff lives in the callers or is punted to the agent loop, so treat that detail as unconfirmed).

Here's my weather before we start circling. Three of these reframe the problem in ways I'd want on the table before any decision; the rest is terrain-mapping.

## 1. Decoupling dissolves a tension the married model has been *paying down by hand*

The strongest pro-decoupling argument isn't merge relief — it's `JJS0:1403`. Pensum seeds were evicted from gallops into officium-local storage because *"each mint committed to gallops, advancing HEAD past origin and triggering the curia readiness guard on subsequent relays."*

That is the marriage biting its own tail. In the married model, JJ's bookkeeping commits and the work repo's git-state preconditions (`jjx_relay` demands `HEAD == origin/branch`, clean tree) contend for the *same HEAD*. The pensum-seed migration was a symptom; decoupling is the cure. Once JJ commits land in a *separate* JJ repo, the work repo's HEAD only ever advances from real work, and the contamination class disappears — not just the pensum instance, every future instance of it. I'd weight this **above** merge relief in the case for decoupling. (High confidence.)

## 2. The merge pressure, the lock bottleneck, and the round-trip friction are *one* root, not three

All three trace to **single-JSON-blob storage**. One file means: any two concurrent writers conflict on it (merge), one global ref must serialize all writers (lock granularity), and every save reserializes-and-byte-compares the *whole* structure (validation cost grows with total size). 

This collapses two of your bullets into one question. "Does the commit-lock bottleneck?" and "keep git or go transactional?" are the **same axis** viewed from two ends. So I'd resist treating the backing-store decision as separable from concurrency — you already sensed this ("tie that decision to the concurrency analysis"); I'd go further and say they're not tied, they're identical. (High confidence on the shared root; the remedy is where I hold looser — see §5.)

## 3. You already have the (repo, SHA) reference type — twice. Generalize, don't invent.

Per load-bearing-complexity: don't mint a new cross-repo-reference machinery when two exist.

- **`basis`** (`jjdcm_basis`, `jjgtn_basis`): every tack *already* records the 7-char HEAD SHA at snapshot time, with `0000000` as the honest "couldn't determine" sentinel. This is precisely "the work-repo SHA this docket was written against" — today it just happens to be the *same* repo. Your proposal is `basis` becoming `(work_repo_id, sha)`.
- **The legatio/BURN registry**: foray *already* names external targets and operates on their SHAs (`jjx_plant` resets a fundus to an exact commit; bind resolves coordinates through a BURN alias). Your "work-repo registry" is a BURN-registry sibling, minus the SSH transport.

So the multi-repo move is: lift `basis` from scalar-SHA to (registry-key, SHA), and add a work-repo registry shaped like the BURN/legatio table. Both have precedent shapes to copy. (High confidence this is the right framing.)

## 4. The fork that governs your worst failure mode: **advisory vs. authoritative SHA**

"JJ ahead of reality / rebased-away SHA" only hurts in proportion to how hard JJ *binds* to the SHA:

- **Advisory** (what `basis` is today): the SHA is a journal annotation. A rebased-away SHA degrades gracefully — you lose exact-diff reconstruction for that tack, JJ state stays valid. The `0000000` sentinel already proves the system tolerates "no SHA."
- **Authoritative**: JJ refuses to operate when the recorded SHA is unreachable. Now you need active reconciliation (detect orphaned SHA → repoint or quarantine), and the dual-commit ordering becomes load-bearing for correctness, not just for tidiness.

My instinct: **keep it advisory by default**, and let foray's existing curia-readiness guard be the *only* place a SHA is authoritative (it already refuses to dispatch against an unpushed/dirty state — generalize that guard to "the work-repo SHA I'm about to record is reachable in the work repo's origin"). That gives you a clean dual-commit discipline — *work-repo-commit-and-push first, then JJ records a SHA it has just verified is durable* — without making every pace fragile to a rebase. Idempotency falls out for free: re-recording the same SHA is a no-op tack. (Medium-high confidence; this is the first place I'd want your pushback, because it trades some referential strictness for resilience and you may want the strictness.)

## 5. Where I land on the backing store — and where I hold loosely

My honest lean: **shard the git-stored JSON before you reach for a transactional store.**

- Per-heat files (`jjg_<firemark>.json`, mirroring the existing per-heat paddock-file scheme `jjp_<encoded>.md`) directly relieve all three §2 pains: different heats → different files → no merge conflict; the lock ref shards to `refs/vvg/locks/jj/<firemark>`; round-trip validation shrinks to per-heat.
- Git stays the source of truth, so the *covenant* survives intact: the `jjb:BRAND:IDENTITY:ACTION:` parseable journal, `jjx_log`'s grep-reconstruction, the forbidden-git-commands additive-only safety model, and free immutability all keep working. A transactional store would make you *re-establish* every one of those in the new substrate.
- Cross-heat ops (draft/restring/relocate) are the real cost: they touch multiple shards, so they either take a lock-ordering protocol or fall back to the global lock. But a single git commit is *already* multi-file-atomic, so the *durability* is free — only the *locking* needs care. That's classic hierarchical locking, well-understood.

Two things that sharpen the transactional-store decision rather than settle it:

- **The "git + derived query index" option addresses the wrong axis.** Your pains are all *write-side* (merge, lock, validation). A read index speeds queries — which aren't slow today (small data). So I'd defer that one until reads actually hurt, and not let it masquerade as a fix for write contention.
- **Tie the transactional store strictly to `jjdk_sole_operator`.** Under the sole-operator premise, concurrency is bounded by "how many chat windows can one human drive" — single digits, millisecond lock holds. A global try-lock is *fine* for that; sharding is more than enough headroom. The transactional store earns its complexity **only if that premise breaks** — a multi-operator or server-hosted singleton JJ. So the real question I'd put to you is: *is the singleton-central-JJ you're imagining still sole-operator, or is it quietly a multi-tenant service?* That answer, not the merge pain, decides git-vs-SQLite. (This is my lowest-confidence area — if you have multi-operator or scale ambitions I haven't sized, the calculus flips and I'd want to hear it.)

---

**Where I'd circle first**, if you want my sequencing lean: nail down §4 (advisory vs. authoritative) and the sole-operator question at the end of §5 *before* anything else — they're cheap to decide and they constrain everything downstream (the registry shape, the reconciliation burden, the storage substrate). The sharding-vs-transactional choice (§5) is genuinely downstream of the premise answer, so deciding it first would be building on sand.

I'm fairly sure §1 and §3 are settled ground we can build on. I'm holding §4 and the §5 premise question open as *yours* to circle. Where do you want to push? And is any of my framing already wrong against intentions you haven't said out loud yet?

aiTitle: Decouple Job Jockey from work repositories

content: We're designing how to decouple Job Jockey from the work repo into a standalone JJ tracking work-repo SHAs. I've laid out my analysis and am waiting on your two upstream decisions: advisory vs. authoritative SHA, and whether the central JJ stays sole-operator. (disable recaps in /config)

message.content: Hah, you've landed on an interesting fault line. curia/fundus concepts led me to develop BURN/BURP concepts, and I'm not sure which is better... but this direction in job jockey certainly recasts the question of where is the knowledge of what workstations are accessible and usable in a devleopment scenario, perhaps across different projects?  That ought not be in the repo-under-surgery.  Thoughts?

content.text:
Yes — and I think the fault line is sharper than "JJ should decouple." You've found that **the same move repeats one layer down**, and the thing that's been quietly mis-homed isn't just initiative-tracking, it's the operator's model of their own fleet.

Let me give you the precise version, because I think the existing design already did most of the factoring for you and you can just *read it off* rather than invent it.

**The legatio's `(alias, reldir)` pair is the factoring, and it tells you exactly what leaves and what stays.**

JJS0 says the legatio's meaningful identity is `(alias, reldir)`, where the alias resolves a BURN and the reldir is the project path on the fundus. Those two components come from *two different scopes*:

- **alias → BURN/BURP → operator topology.** "What machines exist, what accounts reach them, what they're called." This is operator-scoped, identical across every project you touch, and it's the part that ought not be in any work repo. This is the answer to your "what workstations are accessible" question.
- **reldir → project location on a node.** "recipemuster lives at `projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster` on bujn-winpc." This is a *fact about the project* — where it lands, which nodes it tests against. This one is correctly project-scoped and *stays* with the project.

So the answer isn't "station knowledge leaves the repo" — it's that station knowledge was two things glued together, and the join is the legatio. The topology half leaves; the project↔node binding half stays. You don't have to design that seam; it's already load-bearing in the identity.

**curia/fundus vs BURN/BURP isn't "which is better" — they're orthogonal, and the unease is because both got entangled with "model of a remote machine."**

Disentangled:
- **BURN/BURP = the machine as a *stored fact*** (persistent registry — nouns).
- **curia/fundus = the machine's *role in a live operation*** (relative directionality — who invokes, who executes; the same node can be either).
- **legatio/pensum = ephemeral handles** that *realize* a role by resolving the registry.

Neither is better; each is a distinct layer and all three are load-bearing. The reason it *felt* like a competition is that curia/fundus introduced directionality that the symmetric BURN registry lacks, so they look like rival "machine models" when they're really registry-vs-role-vs-handle.

**The consequence I'd weight heaviest: BURN/BURP is consumed beyond JJ, so JJ must *defer* to it, not *absorb* it.**

The moorings tree (`rbmn_`/`rbmu_`) is RBK/BUK substrate — garrison, caparison, invigilate, and ad-hoc SSH all resolve through it; foray is just one more consumer. That means when this topology comes out of the repo, it should land in an **operator-scoped substrate that any project — and any tool, not only JJ — can reference**, and JJ-foray reaches it exactly the way legatio already does: resolve-by-alias, own nothing. The mistake to avoid is letting the JJ-decoupling momentum pull BURN/BURP *into* the JJ singleton as JJ-owned state. JJ borrows the fleet; it doesn't own it. (The legatio being explicitly "a convenience handle, not a persistent record" is the spec already telling you this.)

One precision on your phrasing, in the spirit of not smoothing: it's not literally in "the repo-under-surgery" today — it's in recipemuster, which is wearing *two hats* (a project **and** your tool/infra monorepo). The moorings live in the infra hat. The bug only bites when you work a project that *isn't* recipemuster: that project can't foray without taking a dependency on recipemuster's tree. So the real defect is "operator topology is trapped behind one project's clone," and that's true whether or not that project is under active surgery.

---

Where this points next — and I'll flag it rather than resolve it, because it's a genuine fork and I think it's the *next* fault line, not this one:

Decoupling the JJ store from the work repo can also split **curia (where Claude runs) from the JJ-store-home (where gallops lives)**. Today those are married too. If the singleton JJ store is *central* rather than per-station-local, then the curia reaches it through a transport that starts to look suspiciously like foray-in-reverse — and *that* store's location is itself a piece of operator topology. There's a recursion lurking: the registry of "where my stuff lives" may want to include "where my JJ store lives." I don't think you have to settle it now, but it interacts with the topology home — so I'd hold the question "is the singleton JJ store local-per-station or central?" right next to "where does BURN/BURP go?" and decide them together.

Is the operator-topology-leaves-the-repo framing landing the way you meant it — or were you reaching for something where JJ *does* become the home of fleet knowledge, and I'm talking you out of something you actually want?

Gads I hate the notion of multiple repos but the shape of a thing that stores the memory of currently available hardware just looks like a repo that has a shape job jockey knows about and can interact with.  Thoughts?  Also, suggest a silks like name I can 'rename' this chat to for later consideration, I need to go soon.

content.text:
The reframe that I think dissolves the aversion: **you don't have multiple repos in your future — you have one *fewer* pattern of entanglement.** Right now JJ is smeared across *every* project repo you've married it to. The decoupling doesn't add repos; it *consolidates* N-married-copies into one operator-home store. The thing you hate (JJ state scattered everywhere) is the status quo. The count goes from "1 per project, forever growing" to "+1, total, ever."

And that +1 *should* be repo-shaped — for the exact reason from our first exchange: git's value here is the **journal**, not the storage. "What hardware was available when" wants additive history and diffs just as much as "what was the plan when" does. So a fleet-topology store that's repo-shaped isn't a concession to sprawl; it's you wanting the journal property again. The alternative is a flat dotfile in `~/.config` — which buys you "not a repo" at the cost of losing the very journal you keep reaching for everywhere else. Given how hard the whole system leans on git-as-journal, the dotfile would be the *inconsistent* choice, not the repo.

The clean shape, then: **one operator-home repo, cloned once per station, that no project contains and every project defers to** — co-tenant home for both the singleton JJ gallops *and* the BURN/BURP fleet topology. Co-residence, not ownership merger: BUK still owns topology, JJ still owns gallops, they just share a roof. That also collapses last turn's recursion — "where does my JJ store live" and "where does my fleet live" become the same answer. JJ knows that repo's shape and interacts with it; that's exactly the instinct you just voiced.

So: not "multiple repos." One operator-scoped home, repo-shaped because you want the journal, that ends the per-project smearing.

---

**Silks to rename the chat:** `decouple-jj-and-fleet-into-operator-home`

(If you want it shorter/more findable later: `unmarry-jj-from-work-repo`. The first captures where we *landed*; the second captures what you'll *search for*.)

Go — this'll keep. When you're back, the first thing to circle is still the §4/§5 fork from earlier (advisory-vs-authoritative SHA, and is the singleton store local-per-station or central) — and now "central" has a concrete shape: the operator-home repo.

I wonder that this 'worksite' repo ought to be fully managed by job jockey for consistency?  Always git push, for example, strong git pull discipline too, so that separate job jockey installations can share it

content.text:
It's the right instinct *and* it's idiomatic — JJ already forbids you from hand-managing its git (the whole `jjx_record`/forbidden-git-commands discipline), so "JJ also owns push/pull" is just extending the existing stance: the human never touches the worksite repo's git, JJ does it all. And it cleanly resolves the fork I left open — you're answering "central/shared, synced over git remote," not "local-per-station." Good; that's a real decision, noted.

But here's the sincere hard part, and it's the one that bites: **your commit-lock doesn't span stations.** `refs/vvg/locks/vvx` is a *local* ref — it serializes officia on *one* working tree. Station A's lock is invisible to station B. So "always push" between two stations against a single-blob gallops is the *exact* merge pressure you decoupled to escape — just relocated from officium-vs-officium to station-vs-station. You'd flee the marriage and remarry the network.

The resolution that actually gives you what you're asking for: **git push is itself an atomic compare-and-swap on the remote** (non-fast-forward is rejected atomically). So git *can* be your distributed serializer — if the unit of contention is small enough that two stations rarely touch the same file. That's the sharding insight from earlier, now promoted from optimization to **enabling precondition**:

- Shard the store per-heat (mirroring the per-heat paddock files).
- JJ owns a `pull --rebase` → push → retry-on-rejection loop as the distributed commit protocol.
- Two stations working *different heats* touch *different files* → the rebase is conflict-free → the push-rejection/retry loop *is* your cross-station lock, and it works.
- The single JSON blob is the **one thing** that makes this fail (every write contends on one file; every rebase is a 3-way merge of canonical JSON, which rarely re-emerges canonical). So the blob isn't just merge-painful — under shared-sync it's *disqualifying*.

The sharp residual edge, for when you're back: the **root state** — `next_heat_seed`, `heat_order` — is the irreducible shared point even with per-heat shards. Two stations minting a heat concurrently grab the same firemark seed → collision. There's a precedent staring at this: pensum seeds were evicted from gallops precisely because central minting contended, with the spec declaring *"cross-officium seed collision is acceptable"* (JJS0:1405). Same medicine here → **station-partitioned firemark minting** (each install mints in its own subspace, no cross-station coordination). That's the one place "just shard it" isn't enough.

And one reframing of the premise itself: `jjdk_sole_operator` still *holds* — you're one human — but its current *realization* quietly assumed one working tree. Multi-station honors the premise's intent and breaks its mechanism. So the choice that's now front-and-center isn't "git vs transactional store" in the abstract — it's "does the sole-operator mechanism go distributed (sharded git-CAS) or does the store go central-server (real transactions)?" Your "JJ fully manages, git push/pull, shareable" requirement leans hard toward the *former* — git stays truth, JJ owns the sync protocol, sharding makes it safe.

That's the through-line for next session: **shard → station-partitioned minting → JJ-owned pull-rebase-push.** It keeps git-as-journal, gives you the shared worksite, and never makes you hand-manage a repo. Go — it'll keep under the name you gave it.

message.content: This feature set seems to require further git interaction, a 'git push pull' discipline to do the atomic network things, and that is fine with me in concept.  Lets keep working toward a new heat concept for appropriately consolidating and re-focusing BURN/BURP as well as jjk curia/ fundus towards this new model, I am starting to like it.  Thoughts?

content.text:
Good — and I think it's worth naming *why* you're warming to it, because the reason is load-bearing, not mood: the model stops *fighting* git and starts *using* it. CAS-on-push becomes your lock, the journal stays your truth, and the whole thing *consolidates* (one operator-home) instead of *proliferating* (one-per-project). The "further git interaction" you're accepting is real — JJ grows a genuine distributed-sync surface (pull-rebase-push, remote CAS, a conflict policy) on top of today's commit+local-ref-lock — but it's coherent with everything the system already believes. That coherence is the thing you're responding to.

Here's how I'd shape the heat.

## What's already cinched (paddock `## Cinched` material — don't re-litigate)

- JJ state decouples from work repos into a shared singleton **worksite** store.
- **Git stays source-of-truth and journal** — not a transactional store. The covenant (additive-only, parseable commit-as-intent, `jjx_log` reconstruction) survives intact.
- The store is **central/shared across stations**, synced by a **JJ-owned pull-rebase-push** loop; the human never hand-manages that repo's git.
- **Sharding (per-heat) is the enabling precondition**, not an optimization — single-blob is *disqualifying* under shared-sync.
- **BURN/BURP is BUK substrate JJ defers to** (resolve-by-alias, own nothing). It may *co-reside* in the worksite home; it is not *absorbed* into JJ.
- The legatio `(alias, reldir)` factoring stands: **alias → fleet topology** (moves to worksite), **reldir → project** (stays with the project).
- `basis` is the precedent for per-pace `(repo, SHA)` — **generalize it, don't invent**.

## What the heat must still settle (the real forks — these are its early paces)

1. **Advisory vs. authoritative tracked SHA** (the §4 fork). Governs the whole reconciliation burden. Still open; I lean advisory + foray-guard-as-the-only-authoritative-point, but it's yours.
2. **Station-partitioned firemark/coronet minting** — the root-contention escape (`next_heat_seed`/`heat_order` are the irreducible shared point even with shards). Needs a concrete scheme; the pensum-seed "collision acceptable" precedent is the template.
3. **The conflict policy** for when two stations *do* touch the same shard — git-CAS rejects, JJ retries, but what happens to a genuine content conflict on one heat-file?
4. **The naming mint** — "worksite" is your coinage; it needs to survive the minting discipline (namespaces, terminal exclusivity). Note the vocabulary fit: JJ is equestrian, moorings is nautical, "worksite" is neither — *fundus* is already Latin "estate," so there may be a latent "operator's estate/holdings" vocabulary worth a beat. This is itself an early pace, not a throwaway.
5. **curia/fundus re-focus** — my proposed picture: the **curia is a hub with two transports** — foray/SSH outward to *fundi* (work execution), and git outward to the *worksite* (state sync). The open question: is the worksite a *new role* alongside curia/fundus, or *infrastructure the curia owns*? Settling that is the "re-focus" you're after.

## My lean on scope: this is a **spec-first** heat, and the first of a family

The honest first heat is **the conceptual consolidation + voicing it in the spec** — settle the five forks, mint the concepts, restate the layering (BURN/BURP = registry, curia/fundus = roles, legatio = handle, worksite = shared home), define the sync model in JJS0. 

Implementation — sharding the Rust, station-minting, the pull-rebase-push machinery, the actual moorings relocation — is **downstream heats** that *cannot* be "clear and present" until the model sets. I said two turns ago that deciding storage before the premise is building on sand; the same holds here. Bundling implementation now would force pre-baked dockets against unsettled design, which the docket discipline forbids.

**The cross-kit caution, sincerely:** this spans JJK (curia/fundus, gallops, JJS0) *and* BUK/RBK (BURN/BURP moorings). A heat with two kit-owners tends to lose coherence. What *justifies* co-consideration is the shared worksite home — so make the heat's spine **the worksite model**, with BURN/BURP and curia/fundus as two tributaries it unifies. Keep the center conceptual; let each kit's concrete surgery be its own later, kit-scoped heat.

**Done when:** the worksite model is voiced in the spec, the five forks are resolved-and-recorded, the new concepts are minted, the curia↔worksite↔fundus layering is restated, and BUK's deferral relationship is documented — *with implementation explicitly carved out to follow-on heats.*

## Name

For the heat silks, something like `worksite-model-and-fleet-consolidation` or `worksite-spec-and-curia-refocus` (the second foregrounds the re-focus you named).

---

I haven't opened an officium or touched the gallops — you said "work *toward* a new heat concept," which is shaping, not creating. When you're ready to formalize, my proposed next step is: open an officium, scan the corpus for any existing itch/scar on JJ-decoupling or fleet-consolidation so we build on it rather than duplicate, then draft this as a heat + paddock for your review. Want me to do that now, or keep circling the forks in chat first?

Good — these all land cleanly, and #5 is exactly right: holding the naming as a refinement-before-cutting-paces is the disciplined move (don't pre-bake vocabulary into a paddock that hasn't set). Two of your answers also sharpen the model in ways worth noting back to you: the **token/reservation-with-easy-expiry** (#1) is a deliberately *soft* exclusivity — admin-recoverable, not ironclad — which fits sole-operator-for-now perfectly and keeps you out of distributed-consensus quicksand. And #2's instinct — *bracket gallops lifecycle ops in `pull-rebase → lock → mutate → push → unlock`, but let the repos-under-test sync lazily* — is the right asymmetry: the worksite is authoritative and must be tight; the work repos are just SHA-referenced and can be eventually-consistent.

aiTitle: Decouple Job Jockey from work repositories

content.text:
Confirmed against the code, and the reality is cleaner than the question assumes. Here's the precise picture:

**Factual: vvx/vox is *not* in BUK — and "vox" isn't a driver at all.**

- **BUK / RBK = the bash substrate.** `Tools/buk/` is pure bash (tabtargets, dispatch, regimes, BURN/BURP). RBK is bash recipe-bottle on top of it.
- **The git/state machinery is a separate Rust stack:** `Tools/vvc/` (the commit + lock crate — `vvcc_CommitLock` lives here), `Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/` (the gallops/foray engine), compiled and delivered as the **`vvx` binary** via the VOW pipeline (`vow-b` builds `vvr` → installs as `vvx` in VVK bin). The `jjx` MCP tools run *inside* that vvx process.
- **`vox`/VOK is the *vocabulary* kit** (RCG, VLS, VOS0 — `Tools/vok/`), not a runtime driver. So when you said "vvx/vox machinery," the actual worksite-capable machinery is **vvc + jjk → vvx**; vok is compile-time standards, not a thing that drives git.

So the split is real and total: **bash = BUK; the git-state engine = the Rust vvx stack.** Neither lives in the other.

**Two facts from the code that settle your design question:**

1. **BUK does zero git *mutation* today.** Across all of `Tools/buk/`, the only git verb is `git status` (11 calls). No commit, push, pull, rebase, or update-ref anywhere in bash. *Every* git mutation and the lock itself is Rust (`vvc`, shelling `git update-ref refs/vvg/locks/vvx`).
2. **The Rust side already reads BUK's regime artifacts directly as data.** `jjrlg_legatio.rs:zjjrlg_resolve_burn` parses `.buk/users/.../burn.env` itself — it does *not* shell out to a BUK driver. The grain is already: **BUK produces config files at rest; vvx consumes them by direct parse and owns all git + the lock + the canonical JSON.**

**My recommendation: keep the worksite git-engine in vvx; don't grow a BUK git-mutation driver.**

- It honors the existing grain exactly — BUK stays the *semantics owner* for fleet artifacts (what a BURN profile or a reservation token *means*, how to validate one), and vvx stays the *git/state engine* that commits and syncs them, the same way it already owns gallops. BUK writes the file; vvx commits it.
- It avoids the trap your own model created: a co-tenant worksite (gallops + fleet/tokens) with **two** git drivers would race each other *on one station* the same way two stations race — so you'd need them to share a lock anyway. One engine, one lock, no race.
- "Exclusive JSON Ownership" (JJS0:318) forbids bash touching gallops content regardless, so a BUK driver could *never* be the full story — it'd be a happy-path-only thing that punts every gallops conflict back to Rust.
- The "but I don't want to need the Rust binary for a simple token op" worry mostly dissolves: **vvx is already present wherever JJ runs**, and reservations are issued/expired from the *curia* (where vvx lives), not from a bare fundus. I don't see a real context where you must mutate the worksite *without* vvx around.

**The escape hatch — hold it, don't build it.** If a genuine "fleet op must run where vvx isn't" case ever appears, the clean answer is *not* a second bash git driver but a **language-agnostic lock *protocol*** — a spec'd lock-ref + CAS-on-push discipline that any stack can honor — so bash could participate without sharing code or inverting the layer stack (foundation must never depend on the higher Rust layer). That's a fork to record, not settle now — same posture as your #4/#5.

So for the paddock: I'd record **"worksite git-engine = vvx; BUK owns fleet-artifact semantics, not git mutation"** as a recommended early *cinch*, with the lock-as-protocol as a *held* refinement.

That's the last pre-carve question answered. Want me to open an officium now, scan the corpus for any existing itch/scar on JJ-decoupling or fleet-consolidation so we don't duplicate, and draft this as a heat + paddock for your review?

content: Goal: decouple Job Jockey state from work repos into one shared, git-synced operator-home "worksite" repo. We've settled the model and cinched that vvx owns worksite git. Next: open an officium, scan for duplicate heats, and draft the worksite heat plus paddock for your review. (disable recaps in /config)

message.content: I can live with the question of whether to break vvx/vox out of RBK to some other kit being deferred, though it is on my mind.  BUK will be used in places RBK will not, and similarly JJK may also be used independently.  Actually relayering to make JJK more fundamental than RBK might be appropriate.  thoughts?  Yes, good choice to keep BUK from also having mutating operations, but allowing it to have awareness to peek might be good.  And setting up worksite so that multiple discrete projects can share a worksite is good.

content.text:
Good — and there's a distinction hiding in "more fundamental" that's worth making sharp before it sets, because the two senses pull in different directions.

**First, a small factual reconcile on "break vvx/vox out of RBK":** they're already *not* under `rbk/`. The crates sit as siblings — `Tools/vvc/`, `Tools/vok/`, `Tools/jjk/` — not nested in `Tools/rbk/` (only `rbtd`, RBK's own test crate, lives there). So the entanglement you're feeling isn't directory nesting; it's *delivery/repo-home* — the whole Rust stack grew up inside this RBK-centric monorepo and shares its build. That's the real extraction question, and it's fair to defer. I haven't fully mapped the Cargo workspace roots, so I won't overclaim how coupled the *build* is — flagging that as the unmapped part.

**The layering question — two senses of "fundamental," and only one of them applies:**

- **Generality / reuse-scope:** how many contexts use it. Here BUK > JJK > RBK is plainly true — BUK runs where neither does; JJK tracks work on *any* project; RBK only matters where there are recipe-bottles. By this axis, **JJK genuinely is more fundamental than RBK.** Your instinct is right.
- **Dependency direction:** who imports whom. RBK does *not* call JJK and **shouldn't be made to.** Making RBK depend on JJK to express "JJK is more fundamental" would invent a coupling that isn't load-bearing — and worse, it'd mean you could no longer ship RBK to someone who doesn't want initiative-tracking. That's the *inverse* of the goal.

So the relayering that's appropriate lives in **extraction/delivery boundaries, not runtime dependencies**: JJK (+ its Rust core) must be cleanly *standable-alone, without RBK* — while RBK and JJK stay **dependency-independent siblings on BUK**, neither importing the other. That's the same question as your point 1, seen from the JJK side; they're one question.

And note how clean JJK actually is: its *core* (gallops, heats, paces) depends on nothing but `vvc` — it's even **BUK-independent**. Only the *foray* subsystem reaches into BUK's BURN artifacts. So JJK is already most of the way to standalone.

**Why this matters for the heat, concretely:** your point 4 — *multiple discrete projects share one worksite* — is the thing that **operationalizes** JJK's generality. A cross-project worksite is *incoherent* if JJK is welded to RBK. So the worksite work isn't just aesthetically adjacent to the relayering; it *forces* it. The discipline that buys you: the **worksite heat must be RBK-decoupled by design** (no RBK assumptions bake into the model), even though the *physical extraction* of vvx/JJK into its own kit stays a deferred, separate refactor. Record "worksite + JJK are project-agnostic, zero RBK coupling" as a **cinch**; hold the actual extraction as a future heat.

**On "peek" — yes, and here's the scoping that keeps it honest:** BUK reading the worksite is just the *mirror* of what already happens (vvx reads `.buk/burn.env` directly today). So BUK peeking its **own domain** — fleet topology, reservation tokens — is free and natural. The one line not to cross: BUK must **not** parse gallops JSON directly (Exclusive JSON Ownership, and "never reach past the JJK interface to raw storage"). If BUK ever needs gallops *awareness* (e.g. "is a heat active?"), it calls a **vvx read path**, not `jq`. Net shape: **many readers, one writer** — BUK and anything else may peek; only vvx mutates. Reads don't take the lock; a peek tolerates slightly-stale, which fits your soft-reservation model fine.

**On multi-project sharing:** this lands us back on the multi-repo registry from our first exchange — the worksite becomes the operator's cross-project hub (fleet topology + every project's initiative tracking + reservations), and each project is a registered work-repo whose SHAs paces reference (the generalized `basis`). One consequence to *hold, not settle*: gallops may need a **project dimension** (which project owns this heat?) — or projects stay implicit via which work-repo a pace's SHAs come from. That's a real design fork for the heat body, not a pre-carve decision.

---

We've accumulated a solid ledger of cinches and holds across this conversation. My instinct is the next useful move is to make it concrete: open an officium, scan the corpus for any existing itch/scar on JJ-decoupling or fleet-consolidation, and **draft the paddock as exactly that cinch/hold ledger** for your review — shape only, no paces cut yet (honoring your #5). Ready for me to do that, or is there another fork you want to circle first?

input.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣Ba

## Shape

Decouple Job Jockey's state from the work repo and re-home it — together with the
operator's fleet topology — into a single shared **worksite** store (name
provisional): an operator-scoped, repo-shaped home that any number of discrete
projects share. This is a **spec-first** heat. Settle the model and voice it in the
spec(s). Store reshape, minting change, sync machinery, and moorings relocation are
deferred to follow-on heats that cannot be clear-and-present until the model sets.

Spine is the worksite model. BURN/BURP (fleet topology) and curia/fundus (operation
roles) are two tributaries it unifies — kept conceptual here, not refactored.

## Cinched

- **Decouple JJ state from work repos** into a shared singleton worksite. The married
  model contaminates the work repo's git preconditions — the pensum-seed eviction is
  the prior symptom (JJ bookkeeping commits advanced HEAD past origin and tripped
  foray's curia-readiness guard). Decoupling dissolves that contamination class, not
  just one instance. This is the case for decoupling, weighted above merge relief.
- **Git stays source-of-truth and journal — not a transactional store.** The covenant
  survives: additive-only history, parseable commit-as-intent, jjx_log reconstruction,
  free immutability. A transactional store would force re-establishing all of them.
- **Central/shared across stations, synced by a JJ-owned pull-rebase-push loop.** The
  human never hand-manages the worksite's git. Git push is an atomic compare-and-swap
  on the remote; that CAS is the cross-station lock.
- **Per-heat sharding is the enabling precondition, not an optimization.** Single-blob
  storage is the shared root of merge pressure, global-lock contention, and
  round-trip-validation cost — and is disqualifying under shared-sync (every write
  contends on one file; every rebase is a 3-way merge of canonical JSON). Shards let
  two stations on different heats push non-conflicting changes.
- **Exclusivity is soft.** Reservation tokens grant node ownership; operations
  expire/reissue them; an operator can administratively reclaim a forgotten
  reservation. No distributed-consensus machinery — fits sole-operator.
- **Asymmetric sync.** The worksite (authoritative) is tight: gallops lifecycle ops
  bracket as pull-rebase -> lock -> mutate -> push -> unlock. Repos-under-test are
  SHA-referenced only and sync lazily (eventually-consistent).
- **Generalize, don't invent, for cross-repo references.** The basis field (per-tack
  HEAD SHA, with the unknown-sentinel for "can't determine") is already a (repo, SHA)
  reference — lift it to (work-repo, SHA). The legatio/BURN registry already names
  external targets — the work-repo registry is its sibling.
- **The legatio (alias, reldir) pair is the factoring:** alias -> fleet topology
  (leaves the repo, operator-scoped); reldir -> project location (stays with project).
- **BURN/BURP is BUK substrate JJ defers to**, not JJ-owned. It may co-reside in the
  worksite home; it is not absorbed. JJ resolves by alias and owns nothing of it.
- **Worksite git-engine = vvx (Rust); BUK owns fleet-artifact semantics, not git
  mutation.** Verified grain: BUK does no git mutation today (only git status); all
  mutation plus the lock (refs/vvg/locks/vvx) lives in the Rust vvc crate; vvx already
  reads BUK's burn.env artifacts directly. One engine, one lock — two git drivers on a
  co-tenant store would race on a single station the way two stations would.
- **Read/write asymmetry — many readers, one writer.** BUK may peek the worksite, but
  only its own domain (fleet topology, reservation tokens) directly. Gallops awareness
  for BUK routes through a vvx read path — never raw JSON (Exclusive JSON Ownership).
  Reads take no lock and tolerate slight staleness.
- **Worksite + JJ core are project-agnostic, zero RBK coupling.** JJ is more general
  than RBK (used on any tracked project); the right relayering is extraction/delivery
  boundaries, NOT a runtime RBK->JJ dependency. RBK and JJ stay dependency-independent
  siblings on BUK. The cross-project worksite forces this. JJ's core is even
  BUK-independent; only foray reaches BUK artifacts.

## Held (refine before cutting paces)

- **Advisory vs. authoritative tracked SHA.** Lean: advisory by default (degrades
  gracefully under rebase-away, as basis does today), with foray's curia-readiness
  guard the only authoritative point. Governs the entire reconciliation burden.
- **Station-partitioned firemark/coronet minting.** Root state (next_heat_seed,
  heat_order) is the irreducible shared point even with per-heat shards. Needs a
  partition scheme so heat creation requires no cross-station coordination; the
  pensum-seed "cross-officium collision acceptable" stance is the template.
- **Conflict policy** when two stations touch the same shard: CAS rejects, JJ retries a
  small number of times, then recommends an administrative action (still within the
  system). Open: what happens to a genuine content conflict on one heat-file.
- **curia/fundus re-focus.** The curia is a hub with two transports: foray/SSH to fundi
  (work execution), git to the worksite (state). Open: is the worksite a new role
  alongside curia/fundus, or curia-owned infrastructure? Reclaiming/reusing
  curia/fundus is in scope.
- **Project dimension in gallops.** With many projects sharing one worksite: does a
  heat carry an explicit project, or is project implicit via the work-repo its paces'
  SHAs reference?
- **Naming.** "worksite" and any new role term are provisional. Minting (namespace
  enumeration, terminal exclusivity, equestrian/nautical fit — note fundus already =
  Latin "estate") happens once the model sets, not now.
- **Lock-as-protocol escape hatch.** If a real "fleet op must run where vvx is absent"
  context appears, define the worksite lock as a language-agnostic protocol (lock-ref +
  CAS-on-push discipline) so a thin bash driver could participate without code-sharing
  or inverting the layer stack. Hold; do not build speculatively.
- **vvx/JJ extraction from the RBK-centric monorepo** — delivery/workspace decoupling.
  On the operator's mind; deferred.

## Done when

The worksite model is voiced in the spec(s) (JJS0 and/or a new worksite spec): the
Held forks resolved-and-recorded, new concepts minted, the curia<->worksite<->fundus
layering restated, BUK's deferral relationship documented, and implementation
explicitly carved out to named follow-on heats. No store reshape, minting change, sync
machinery, or moorings relocation lands in this heat.

## Character

Design conversation requiring judgment — architectural model-setting, spec-first.
Resist pre-baking implementation; the payoff is a model that sets cleanly so the
downstream implementation heats can be clear-and-present.

- **Cinched (12)** — decouple-dissolves-contamination · git-stays-journal · central/shared JJ-owned pull-rebase-push · per-heat sharding as precondition · soft token exclusivity · asymmetric sync (worksite tight / repos-under-test lazy) · generalize `basis` + work-repo registry · the `(alias, reldir)` factoring · BURN/BURP-as-deferred-substrate · vvx-owns-git-engine / BUK-owns-semantics · many-readers-one-writer peek · project-agnostic zero-RBK-coupling.
- **Held (8)** — advisory-vs-authoritative SHA · station-partitioned minting · same-shard conflict policy · curia/fundus re-focus · project dimension in gallops · naming · lock-as-protocol escape hatch · vvx/JJ monorepo extraction.

And I deliberately kept every line stale-by-mount durable (concept names, not line numbers or counts) so it survives until you next mount it.

input.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣Ba

## Shape

This heat creates the **worksite repo** and the **atomic Rust manipulators** that own
its read/write/commit, and **rebalances the fundus/curia/BURP/BURN files** into the
worksite model (name provisional): an operator-scoped, repo-shaped home that any number
of discrete projects share. It carries an unresolved design core — chiefly the
worksite's git-database representation — and is stood up now to **hold** the decisions
and open questions below until the operator can focus on resolving them. Today's act is
capture, not construction; the build waits until the design core settles.

Spine is the worksite model. BURN/BURP (fleet topology) and curia/fundus (operation
roles) are reorganized into it, not merely referenced.

## Cinched

- **Decouple JJ state from work repos** into a shared singleton worksite. The married
  model contaminates the work repo's git preconditions — the pensum-seed eviction is
  the prior symptom (JJ bookkeeping commits advanced HEAD past origin and tripped
  foray's curia-readiness guard). Decoupling dissolves that contamination class, not
  just one instance. This is the case for decoupling, weighted above merge relief.
- **Git stays source-of-truth and journal — not a transactional store.** The covenant
  survives: additive-only history, parseable commit-as-intent, jjx_log reconstruction,
  free immutability. A transactional store would force re-establishing all of them.
- **Central/shared across stations, synced by a JJ-owned pull-rebase-push loop.** The
  human never hand-manages the worksite's git. Git push is an atomic compare-and-swap
  on the remote; that CAS is the cross-station lock.
- **Writers must be isolatable; single-blob storage is disqualifying.** Single-blob is
  the shared root of merge pressure, global-lock contention, and round-trip-validation
  cost — and under shared-sync it fails outright (every write contends on one file;
  every rebase is a 3-way merge of canonical JSON). Two stations on different heats must
  push without conflict. Per-heat sharding is the natural mechanism; the exact
  representation is the git-database fork (Held) — principle is cinched, mechanism open.
- **Exclusivity is soft.** Reservation tokens grant node ownership; operations
  expire/reissue them; an operator can administratively reclaim a forgotten
  reservation. No distributed-consensus machinery — fits sole-operator.
- **Asymmetric sync.** The worksite (authoritative) is tight: gallops lifecycle ops
  bracket as pull-rebase -> lock -> mutate -> push -> unlock. Repos-under-test are
  SHA-referenced only and sync lazily (eventually-consistent).
- **Generalize, don't invent, for cross-repo references.** The basis field (per-tack
  HEAD SHA, with the unknown-sentinel for "can't determine") is already a (repo, SHA)
  reference — lift it to (work-repo, SHA). The legatio/BURN registry already names
  external targets — the work-repo registry is its sibling.
- **The legatio (alias, reldir) pair is the factoring:** alias -> fleet topology
  (leaves the repo, operator-scoped); reldir -> project location (stays with project).
- **BURN/BURP is BUK substrate JJ defers to**, not JJ-owned. The rebalance relocates
  these files into the worksite home (co-residence), but JJ resolves by alias and owns
  nothing of their semantics — co-residence, not absorption.
- **Worksite git-engine = vvx (Rust); BUK owns fleet-artifact semantics, not git
  mutation.** Verified grain: BUK does no git mutation today (only git status); all
  mutation plus the lock (refs/vvg/locks/vvx) lives in the Rust vvc crate; vvx already
  reads BUK's burn.env artifacts directly. One engine, one lock — two git drivers on a
  co-tenant store would race on a single station the way two stations would.
- **Read/write asymmetry — many readers, one writer.** BUK may peek the worksite, but
  only its own domain (fleet topology, reservation tokens) directly. Gallops awareness
  for BUK routes through a vvx read path — never raw JSON (Exclusive JSON Ownership).
  Reads take no lock and tolerate slight staleness.
- **Worksite + JJ core are project-agnostic, zero RBK coupling.** JJ is more general
  than RBK (used on any tracked project); the right relayering is extraction/delivery
  boundaries, NOT a runtime RBK->JJ dependency. RBK and JJ stay dependency-independent
  siblings on BUK. The cross-project worksite forces this. JJ's core is even
  BUK-independent; only foray reaches BUK artifacts.

## Held (refine before cutting paces)

- **Worksite git-database representation — the design core that gates the build.** A
  peer to the gallops JSON (further JSON stores committed alongside), or something more
  primal (git objects/refs as the database directly). Git-stays-truth holds either way;
  this decision sets the data shape and subsumes the sharding mechanism above.
- **Scope boundary of Ba vs follow-on heats.** Which of {gallops sharding rollout,
  station-partitioned minting, the full cross-station pull-rebase-push sync loop} land
  in this heat versus later. Settled once the git-database fork resolves.
- **Advisory vs. authoritative tracked SHA.** Lean: advisory by default (degrades
  gracefully under rebase-away, as basis does today), with foray's curia-readiness
  guard the only authoritative point. Governs the entire reconciliation burden.
- **Station-partitioned firemark/coronet minting.** Root state (next_heat_seed,
  heat_order) is the irreducible shared point even with per-heat shards. Needs a
  partition scheme so heat creation requires no cross-station coordination; the
  pensum-seed "cross-officium collision acceptable" stance is the template.
- **Conflict policy** when two stations touch the same shard: CAS rejects, JJ retries a
  small number of times, then recommends an administrative action (still within the
  system). Open: what happens to a genuine content conflict on one heat-file.
- **curia/fundus re-focus.** The curia is a hub with two transports: foray/SSH to fundi
  (work execution), git to the worksite (state). Open: is the worksite a new role
  alongside curia/fundus, or curia-owned infrastructure? Reclaiming/reusing
  curia/fundus is in scope.
- **Project dimension in gallops.** With many projects sharing one worksite: does a
  heat carry an explicit project, or is project implicit via the work-repo its paces'
  SHAs reference?
- **Naming.** "worksite" and any new role term are provisional. Minting (namespace
  enumeration, terminal exclusivity, equestrian/nautical fit — note fundus already =
  Latin "estate") happens once the model sets, not now.
- **Lock-as-protocol escape hatch.** If a real "fleet op must run where vvx is absent"
  context appears, define the worksite lock as a language-agnostic protocol (lock-ref +
  CAS-on-push discipline) so a thin bash driver could participate without code-sharing
  or inverting the layer stack. Hold; do not build speculatively.
- **vvx/JJ extraction from the RBK-centric monorepo** — delivery/workspace decoupling.
  On the operator's mind; deferred.

## Done when

The worksite repo exists and its atomic Rust manipulators (validated read/write/commit
plus lock) are built and tested; the fundus/curia/BURP/BURN files are rebalanced into
the worksite model; the git-database representation and the other Held forks are
resolved-and-recorded; and the Ba-vs-follow-on scope boundary is decided. No
construction begins until the design core (the git-database approach) is settled.

## Character

Design requiring judgment, then a careful build — architectural model-setting plus the
worksite repo and its atomic manipulators. Standing now as a holding pen for settled
decisions and open forks; construction waits until the operator can focus and resolve
the git-database core. Resist building before the model sets.

attachment.snippet:
1	## File Acronym Mappings — BUK Subdirectory (`Tools/buk/`)
2	
3	- **BCG**  → `buk/vov_veiled/BCG-BashConsoleGuide.md` (Bash Console Guide - enterprise bash patterns)
4	- **WSG**  → `buk/vov_veiled/WSG-WindowsScriptingGuide.md` (Windows Scripting Guide - ssh-to-Windows transport discipline; extends BCG into the wsl.exe / cygwin / PowerShell stack)
5	- **BUS0** → `buk/vov_veiled/BUS0-BashUtilitiesSpec.adoc` (Bash Utilities Specification - tabtarget dispatch vocabulary)
6	- **BUC**  → `buk/buc_command.sh` (command utilities, buc_* functions)
7	- **BUD**  → `buk/bud_dispatch.sh` (dispatch utilities, zbud_* functions)
8	- **BUG**  → `buk/bug_git.sh` (bash git utilities, bug_* functions — home of the "tools never commit, gate on a clean tree" gate `bug_require_clean_tree`)
9	- **BUH**  → `buk/buh_handbook.sh` (handbook utilities, buh_* functions - always-visible user interaction)
10	- **BUT**  → `buk/but_test.sh` (test utilities, but_* functions)
11	- **BUYM** → `buk/buym_yelp.sh` (yelp module — diastema wire format, yawp functions, format resolver, legacy captures)
12	- **BUV**  → `buk/buv_validation.sh` (validation utilities, buv_* functions)
13	- **BUW**  → `buk/buw_workbench.sh` (workbench utilities, buw_* functions)
14	- **BUTT** → `buk/butt_testbench.sh` (BUK test framework self-test — kick-tires + bure-tweak, 9 cases)
15	- **BURC** → `buk/burc_cli.sh`, `buk/burc_regime.sh` (regime configuration)
16	- **BURS** → `buk/burs_cli.sh`, `buk/burs_regime.sh` (regime station)
17	- **BUJP** → `buk/bujp_preflight.sh` (garrison step-1 preflight gate)
18	
19	## Bash Utility Kit (BUK) Concepts
20	
21	BUK provides tabtarget/launcher infrastructure for bash-based tooling.
22	
23	### TabTarget System
24	
25	TabTargets are lightweight shell scripts in `tt/` that serve as the CLI entry point for all operations. They delegate to workbenches via launchers — no business logic lives in tabtargets.
26	
27	**Discoverability**: `ls tt/` shows all available commands. Tab completion narrows by prefix: `tt/rbw-<TAB>`.
28	
29	**Naming pattern**: `{colophon}.{frontispiece}[.{imprint}].sh`
30	
31	| Part | Purpose | Example |
32	|------|---------|---------|
33	| **Colophon** | Routing identifier (includes hyphen, workbench matches on this) | `rbw-cr`, `buw-tt-ll` |
34	| **Frontispiece** | Human-readable description (PascalCase) | `Rack` |
35	| **Imprint** | Optional target parameter (nameplate moniker, fixture name, etc.) | `tadmor` |
36	
37	- The `.` is the delimiter between parts
38	- The hyphen is part of the colophon (not a separator)
39	
40	Example: `tt/rbw-cr.Rack.tadmor.sh` — colophon `rbw-cr` routes to the crucible rack command, frontispiece tells you what it does, imprint `tadmor` selects the nameplate.
41	
42	Multiple tabtargets can share the same colophon but differ by imprint:
43	```
44	tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh
45	tt/rbw-cC.Charge.srjcl.sh
46	tt/rbw-cC.Charge.pluml.sh
47	```
48	
49	### BUK Vocabulary
50	
51	| Term | Definition |
52	|------|------------|
53	| **Zipper** | BCG-compliant module kindling colophon→module→command array constants |
54	| **Workbench** | Routes commands: `{prefix}w_workbench.sh` |
55	| **Testbench** | Routes tests: `{prefix}t_testbench.sh` |
56	| **Folio** | Runtime target value (`BUZ_FOLIO`) passed to a command — nameplate moniker, role name, etc. How it arrives depends on the channel |
57	| **Channel** | Enrollment-time declaration of how a colophon receives its folio: `imprint` (from filename — one tabtarget per target), `param1` (command-line argument — single tabtarget), or empty (no folio needed) |
58	
59	**Key files:**
60	- `Tools/buk/buc_command.sh` — command utilities
61	- `Tools/buk/bud_dispatch.sh` — dispatch utilities
62	- `Tools/buk/buw_workbench.sh` — workbench
63	
64	Full spec: `Tools/buk/README.md`
65	
66	## Forbidden Shell Operations
67	
68	**Never use `cd` in Bash commands — NO exceptions.**
69	
70	The working directory persists between Bash tool calls. A single `cd` corrupts ALL subsequent commands that use relative paths, including every `./tt/` tabtarget.
71	
72	- Use absolute paths instead of cd'ing
73	
74	**There is no safe cd.** Do not reason that "I'll cd back" — the next tool call may be yours or another Claude Code session's, and it will break.
75	
76	## Tool Git Discipline
77	
78	Tools never commit in the consumer's codebase. A tool MAY presume git and refuse downstream steps on a dirty tree, but it never stages or commits — the operator commits with their usual workflow. The uniform gate is `bug_require_clean_tree "<operation>"` (BUG module); a verb that installs into tracked config calls it first, so an install-then-forgot-to-commit cannot silently ride into a later build.
79	
80	## TabTarget Invocation Discipline
81	
82	**Never wrap tabtarget invocations with `tee`, `tail`, `head`, `grep`, `2>&1`, or any other pipe — NO exceptions.**
83	
84	Tabtargets self-log to `../logs-buk/`:
85	- `last.txt` — most recent invocation across all tabtargets
86	- `same-{cmd}.txt` — most recent invocation of this specific tabtarget
87	- `hist-{cmd}-{timestamp}.txt` — historical invocations
88	
89	Both stdout and stderr are captured. Adding your own `tee` or `2>&1` duplicates work the tool already did. The real hazard is piping a tabtarget into `tail`/`head`/`grep`: zsh defaults `pipefail` OFF, so the pipeline returns the last command's exit status — usually 0. **A failing test reports as success.**
90	
91	- Run the tabtarget directly, then read from `../logs-buk/` in a separate command
92	- Environment variables before the command are fine: `BURE_CONFIRM=skip ./tt/foo.sh`
93	- If you genuinely must pipe live output (rare — usually you can read the log instead), set `-o pipefail` on the same line, or check `${PIPESTATUS[0]}` (bash) / `${pipestatus[1]}` (zsh)
94	
95	```
96	# Wrong — exit code is from `tail`, not the tabtarget; failures masked
97	./tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh 2>&1 | tee /tmp/log | tail -80
98	
99	# Wrong — even a bare `| head` discards the real signal
100	./tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh | head -50
101	
102	# Right — separate commands; exit code preserved
103	./tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh
104	tail -80 ../logs-buk/last.txt
105	```
106	
107	**There is no safe `tee | tail`.** Do not reason "I'll just truncate the output for readability" — the truncation and the exit-code-eating are inseparable. Truncate by reading the log file afterward.
108	
109	## Test Execution Discipline
110	
111	Run test fixture tabtargets **sequentially, never in parallel**. Test fixtures share regime state and container/network namespaces — parallel execution causes resource conflicts and false failures.
112	
113	```
114	# Correct: run one at a time
115	tt/rbw-tf.TestFixture.regime-validation.sh
116	tt/rbw-tf.TestFixture.tadmor-security.sh
117	
118	# Wrong: never run fixtures concurrently
119	tt/rbw-tf.TestFixture.regime-validation.sh & tt/rbw-tf.TestFixture.tadmor-security.sh &
120	```

1	## Job Jockey Configuration
2	
3	Job Jockey (JJ) is installed for managing project initiatives.
4	
5	**Concepts:**
6	- **Heat**: Bounded initiative with coherent goals that are clear and present (3-50 officia). Status: `racing` (active execution) or `stabled` (paused for planning). Location: `current/` or `retired/` (done).
7	- **Pace**: Discrete action within a heat.
8	- **Itch**: Future work (any detail level), lives in jji_itch.md
9	- **Scar**: Closed work with lessons learned, lives in jjs_scar.md
10	- **Spook**: Team infrastructure stumble — any workflow failure improvable with deft attention. Capture as a pace when encountered, don't lose the current thread.
11	- **Cinch**: A decision settled in a paddock or docket and not to be re-litigated — both noun and verb ("cinch the approach," "the inscribe-skip is cinched"). Distinct from a *lock* in the concurrency sense (JJK's git-ref commit lock), which keeps that word.
12	
13	**Sincerity over efficiency:** When you notice something — a pattern, a concern, an insight about the work or the collaboration itself — say it. Discovery through conversation is part of the work, not a detour from it.
14	
15	**Docket posture:** A docket is a specification of needed change, not architectural commentary. It articulates what done looks like and any cinched constraints; it points at sources rather than restating them. A short `## Character` line naming the cognitive posture (e.g., "intricate but mechanical," "design conversation requiring judgment") earns its keep; the rest of the docket should resist filling in.
16	
17	**Slate-time vs mount-time.** The slate agent has just done analysis; the mount agent will reorient against the project as it stands then, with CLAUDE.md and specs already loaded. Hand off goal and boundary, not the analysis. Depth belongs in the slate commit message, not the docket body.
18	
19	**Stale-by-mount filter.** Each line must still be true at mount-time. Fail (drift): line numbers, byte/match counts, version strings, exhaustive site enumerations. Pass (durable): file paths as entry points, commit hashes, spec/concept names.
20	
21	**Docket anti-patterns:**
22	- Restating commits/specs the mount agent already loads. Point, don't paraphrase.
23	- Pre-baking implementation (function names, signatures, locations for new code). Mount-time decisions.
24	- Site enumerations. Write a discovery recipe (`grep "pattern" Tools/rbk/`) instead of listing six file:line entries.
25	- Body over ~15 lines warrants suspicion of overprescription.
26	- **Plan-step structure stays in the plan.** Docket phase/step labels (A/B/C, "Phase 1", "first/then/finally") must not appear as code comments — the plan won't exist at maintenance time, and line order already conveys execution.
27	- **Absolute paths to working trees.** Don't pin a docket to a specific repo clone (`/Users/foo/projects/your-project`, `~/proj/`). Mount may legitimately run against a different clone — if work needs to happen in *the* working directory, say so without naming it. Cross-repo coupling, if real, belongs in the paddock as a heat-shape note, not pace prose. Absolute paths *are* acceptable for external data roots (log dirs, `/tmp/...` scratch, machine-pinned data) — but flag those as operator-mutable rather than identity.
28	
29	**Reference discipline.** Pace order is the dependency tree — single-operator workflow runs paces in heat order, so explicit dependency markers in docket prose are usually overspecified. Coronet cross-refs in dockets earn their keep only when the dependency crosses heats or skips order — rare, not never.
30	
31	**Paddock posture.** A paddock articulates shape, cinched decisions, and what done looks like — not a progress journal. Git log and `jjx_log` are the journal; the paddock is the shape. **Coronets do not appear in paddock prose** — not retrospectively (annotating landed work, e.g., "BBAAM depot-identity-collapse"), not prospectively (naming planned paces, e.g., "AAF — Docker dual-daemon"), not as cross-references. A coronet is an enrollment-ledger key, not shape data; pace-state operations (drop, relabel, reorder, transfer) silently invalidate coronet refs while the prose keeps them. Refer to paces by purpose, not identifier; `jjx_show` is the authoritative source for what paces exist. Firemarks may appear (heats change rarely). When editing a paddock for other reasons, prune any coronet refs you find.
32	
33	**Mid-execution posture.** When a failure or surprise surfaces while a pace is mounted, the default response is mechanism + one specific repair you'd attempt. Do not proliferate options or weigh consequences across alternatives — pace scope and segmentation are operator territory. If the repair is obvious, proceed; if not, surface the question and stop.
34	
35	**Identities vs Display Names:**
36	- **Firemark**: Heat identity, two characters from a nonstandard b64 alphabet (e.g., `₣AA`, `₣Av`, `₣A-`, `₣A_`, `₣A7`). Used in command params and JSON keys.
37	- **Coronet**: Pace identity, firemark plus three characters from the same alphabet (e.g., `₢AAAAk`, `₢A-AAm`, `₢A_A-p`, `₢AAA_7`). Used in command params and JSON keys.
38	- **Silks**: kebab-case display name. Human-readable only — NOT usable for lookups.
39	
40	When a command takes a firemark or coronet, provide the identity, not the silks.
41	
42	**Display discipline (agent text output to user):**
43	- **Always show full coronets and firemarks; never abbreviate.** Write
44	  `₢A-AA-`, never `AA-`. Write `₣A-`, never `A-`. The operator works in
45	  several heats simultaneously; a bare 3-char pace suffix is ambiguous and
46	  forces re-derivation of the heat from context. The ₣/₢ glyph + heat half
47	  is part of the identity, not decoration. This holds in prose, tables,
48	  bullets, headers, and casual back-references.
49	- **Prefer coronets over silks in references.** Coronets are precise and
50	  immutable; silks change via `jjx_relabel`. Lead with the coronet; silks
51	  may follow parenthetically for human readability (e.g.,
52	  `₢A-AA- (wsg-trim-to-spec-shape)`). The coronet alone is always
53	  sufficient; the silks alone never are.
54	
55	**Case sensitivity**: Firemarks and coronets are case-sensitive. `Av` ≠ `AV` ≠ `av`. Passing the wrong case produces a confusing "not found" error. Copy identities exactly as displayed — the final character's case distinguishes heats (e.g., `₣Av` vs `₣AV` are different heats).
56	
57	- Target repo dir: `.`
58	- JJ Kit path: `Tools/jjk/README.md`
59	
60	**MCP Tool Usage:**
61	
62	All JJK commands are accessed via the single `mcp__vvx__jjx` MCP tool with four parameters:
63	- `command`: string selecting the operation — always the canonical `jjx_*` name (e.g., `"jjx_show"`, `"jjx_enroll"`, `"jjx_record"`)
64	- `params`: JSON object with command-specific fields (see reference below)
65	- `officium`: officium identity string from `jjx_open` (required on all commands except `jjx_open` — see Officium Protocol below)
66	- `model`: agent's verbatim model ID string from its system prompt (e.g., `"claude-opus-4-8"`). Required on ALL commands including `jjx_open`. The server gates commands by model tier — currently all commands require opus.
67	
68	**`params` must be a JSON object, never a string.** If params is accidentally stringified (e.g., `"{\"key\": \"val\"}"` instead of `{"key": "val"}`), deserialization will fail. The server has a defensive fallback for this, but always pass a native object.
69	
70	**Verb names are NOT command names**: there is no `jjx_slate`, `jjx_mount`, `jjx_notch`, `jjx_groom` command. The verb table below maps horse vocabulary to actual MCP commands.
71	NEVER invent param fields — check the reference below first.
72	
73	**Quick Verbs** — When user says just the verb, invoke the corresponding command:
74	
75	| Verb | Noun | MCP command |
76	|------|------|-------------|
77	| muster | heats | `jjx_list` |
78	| parade | heat/pace | `jjx_show` |
79	| scout | heats | `jjx_search` |
80	| nominate | heat | `jjx_create` |
81	| mount | heat/pace | See Mount Protocol below |
82	| groom | heat | See Groom Protocol below |
83	| slate | pace | `jjx_enroll` |

... [232 lines truncated] ...

45	`<your-project-reldir>`; the rbm-specific value is `projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster`.
46	
47	### C. Build & Run Discipline (rbm/VOK-specific)
48	
49	**Build & Run Discipline:**
50	Always run these after Rust code changes:
51	- `tt/vow-b.Build.sh` — Build
52	- `tt/vvw-r.RunVVX.sh` — Run VVX
53	
54	### D. Gradient delivery — mount and groom
55	
56	Governs the Mount and Groom protocols in `claude-jjk-core.md`: when those
57	protocols say to surface the docket, approach, or paddock, this is *how* to
58	surface them.
59	
60	Mount and groom surface heat artifacts (paddock, docket) written AI-for-AI and
61	dense by default; the operator does not read dockets directly and may be tired.
62	This is a *craft constraint on presentation, not a cap on thinking* — hold the
63	full analysis internally; shape the delivery so the operator has a place to
64	stand and a way to climb. Default, not gated on how long ago the pace was
65	slated: a same-session AI-dense docket is already a wall by afternoon.
66	
67	At mount and groom:
68	
69	- **Lead with one sentence the operator can stand on** — the pace goal (mount)
70	  or heat state (groom) — then *stop and let them pull the rest down*. Do not
71	  follow the opening sentence with the full docket, the approach, and every
72	  flagged concern in one turn. That sequence is the wall.
73	- **One path fully; alternatives in a line.** Present the single recommended
74	  path in full; give each alternative one line the operator can ask you to
75	  expand. Three fully-detailed options is word-wall in the costume of
76	  thoroughness.
77	- **Depth on request, in layers.** The operator throttles — "more," "the
78	  detail," "the others" pulls the next layer.
79	
80	Shared antipattern names (use as shorthand, either direction):
81	
82	- **Stun** — a wall of dense, fully-structured prose with no on-ramp;
83	  comprehension seizes before it can enter. The finished staircase, seen from
84	  outside, is a wall.
85	- **Molehill** — manufacturing a significant-looking decision-fork or challenge
86	  out of something nearly inconsequential, then building structure around it.
87	  Before presenting a decision or concern, weigh its real stakes; if small,
88	  treat it in a clause, not a fork. Unsure if it's a molehill? Say so in a line
89	  rather than building it up.

aiTitle: Decouple Job Jockey from work repositories

content.text:
This is the exact failure mode the `₣Ba` heat exists to kill — concurrent officia writing the single-blob gallops, now colliding on rebase. Live evidence for the design, ironically arriving mid-conversation. Let me assess before touching anything.

First, important boundary I'll hold: I will **not** hand-edit the gallops JSON (Exclusive JSON Ownership forbids it) and I will **not** use any forbidden git verb (reset/restore/checkout-file/stash/clean/rebase --skip — the last would *drop* another officium's commit, violating additive-only). I'll diagnose, then recommend, and ask before any mutating step. Reading the conflict to understand it is diagnosis, not authoring — that I'll do.

input.command: echo "=== git status ==="; git -C /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster status; echo; echo "=== unmerged files ==="; git -C /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster diff --name-only --diff-filter=U; echo; echo "=== conflict markers in gallops ==="; grep -nE '^(<<<<<<<|=======|>>>>>>>)' /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/.claude/jjm/jjg_gallops.json; echo; echo "=== the commit being replayed (theirs) ==="; git -C /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster log -1 --format='%H%n%s%n%n%b' e7d0ac456
input.description: Assess rebase state, conflict location, conflicting commit
